metadata.summary: Use zero-runtime libraries for RSC; runtime only for client SPAs. Define styled at module scope, use transient props, augment TypeScript for theme safety.

content.title: CSS-in-JS
content.summary: A methodology for applying CSS-in-JS in React projects: use zero-runtime libraries (`vanilla-extract`, `linaria`, `panda-css`) for RSC and SSR builds; runtime libraries (`styled-components` v6, `@emotion/styled`) only for client-only SPAs. Define styled components at module scope, never inside the function body. Use transient props (`$variant`, `$size`), co-locate tokens with TypeScript augmentation, and extract critical CSS for SSR to prevent FOUC. Runtime CSS-in-JS is incompatible with React Server Components and adds 10-30 KB of runtime overhead before any component styles. Defining `styled.X` inside render creates a new class on every render, causing class explosion and performance degradation.

why.p: Runtime CSS-in-JS is incompatible with React Server Components and adds 10-30 KB of runtime overhead before any component styles. Defining `styled.X` inside render creates a new class on every render, causing class explosion and performance degradation. Without `$transient` props, style props leak to the DOM. Without TypeScript theme augmentation, token typos are undetected. These failures are silent until production.

list.item: React or Vue components with truly dynamic styles computed from runtime props and state.
Design systems where tokens need TS-typed access from inside components.
A11y-driven theming (dark mode, high-contrast, prefers-reduced-motion).
Component libraries shipped to npm expecting zero CSS-import setup.
Migrating from inline `style={}` blobs to scoped maintainable styles.
Next.js 15 and App Router with RSC: runtime CSS-in-JS breaks Server Components. Use Tailwind, CSS Modules, or `vanilla-extract`.
Static marketing sites where CSS Modules or plain CSS ship less JS.
Tailwind-standardized teams. Mixing creates a split design system.
Embedded or size-constrained bundles (extension popups, AMP, email).
React Native. Use StyleSheet API; CSS-in-JS libs that map onto RN carry maintenance risk.

rule.statement: In Next.js 13 and later App Router with React Server Components, use zero-runtime CSS-in-JS only: vanilla-extract, linaria, or panda-css. Never use styled-components or @emotion/styled runtime libraries in Server Components.
rule.rationale: Runtime CSS-in-JS requires JavaScript to generate styles at render time. Server Components render on the server without a browser JS context; adding runtime CSS-in-JS either throws at build time or produces hydration mismatches.

rule.statement: In Vite SPAs and client-only React apps, styled-components v6 or @emotion/styled are acceptable if the team is not already on Tailwind.
rule.rationale: Without the RSC constraint, runtime CSS-in-JS works correctly and provides co-located, typed styles. The primary cost is runtime overhead and bundle size.

rule.statement: Do not install both styled-components and @emotion/styled in the same project.
rule.rationale: Both export a css tag and a styled factory. Mixed imports cause silent style conflicts and inflate the bundle with two runtime engines.

rule.statement: Define styled components at module scope, never inside a function component body.
rule.rationale: styled.div inside a component body creates a new class name on every render, causing class explosion, style inconsistency, and performance degradation that grows with re-render frequency.

rule.statement: Use the $ prefix for props passed to styled components that should not propagate to the DOM: $variant, $size, $fullWidth.
rule.rationale: Without the $ prefix, custom props are forwarded to the DOM element and trigger React warnings about unknown attributes (variant="primary" on a button). Transient props are consumed by styled-components and stripped before DOM rendering.

rule.statement: Augment the DefaultTheme interface for styled-components: declare module 'styled-components' { interface DefaultTheme extends Theme {} }. This is mandatory when using theme tokens in components.
rule.rationale: Without the augmentation, theme property access (theme.colors.primary) is typed as any. TypeScript cannot catch token name typos, and refactoring tokens requires manual grep instead of compiler-driven updates.

rule.statement: In client-side rendering frameworks with SSR (Next.js Pages Router, Gatsby), configure the CSS-in-JS library's SSR extraction: ServerStyleSheet for styled-components, extractCriticalToChunks for Emotion.
rule.rationale: Without SSR extraction, the HTML arrives without styles. The browser renders the unstyled page, then applies styles after the JS bundle hydrates — a Flash Of Unstyled Content (FOUC) visible on every first load.

section.name: examples
section.p: Concrete implementations with styled-components and Emotion.

example.title: Typed Button with Variants (styled-components)
example.note: $variant and $size use $ prefix (transient). Component defined at module scope.

example.title: Theme with TypeScript Augmentation
example.note: The styled.d.ts augmentation is mandatory. Without it, theme properties are typed as any and typos are undetected.

example.title: Emotion css Prop
example.note: @jsxImportSource pragma enables the css prop without Babel config. Emotion reads theme from ThemeContext via useTheme.

antipattern.description: Defining styled.div or styled.button inside a function component body.
antipattern.reason: A new class name is generated on every render call. This causes class explosion in the stylesheet, React DevTools shows hundreds of one-time classes, and the browser must repeatedly re-compute styles. Define styled components at module scope always.

antipattern.description: Using runtime CSS-in-JS (styled-components, @emotion/styled) in a Next.js App Router Server Component without 'use client' directive.
antipattern.reason: Runtime CSS-in-JS requires the browser's JS context to generate styles. Server Components have no client context. The build fails or produces hydration mismatches that are hard to diagnose. Use zero-runtime alternatives or add 'use client' to the component.

antipattern.description: Passing $variant or $size props without the $ prefix: styled.button with variant="primary" on the element.
antipattern.reason: Unknown props are forwarded to the DOM element, triggering React warnings in development and potentially invalid HTML. styled-components strips transient ($-prefixed) props before DOM forwarding.

antipattern.description: Skipping the styled.d.ts TypeScript augmentation for DefaultTheme.
antipattern.reason: Without augmentation, all theme property access is typed as any. Token name typos go undetected until runtime. Renaming a token does not trigger TypeScript errors, and the bug surfaces only as an undefined style value.

antipattern.description: Installing both styled-components and @emotion/styled in the same project.
antipattern.reason: Both export a css helper and a styled factory. Mixed imports at different files cause the wrong css function to be called, producing styles that do not compile or produce unexpected output. Two runtime engines also double the JS overhead.

antipattern.description: Hardcoding color literals inside styled templates: background: #3b82f6 instead of ${({ theme }) => theme.colors.primary}.
antipattern.reason: Hardcoded colors bypass the token system. Theme changes, dark mode, and brand updates require grep-and-replace across all components instead of a single token update.

antipattern.description: Skipping SSR style extraction in frameworks with server rendering (Next.js Pages Router, Gatsby).
antipattern.reason: Without extracting critical CSS server-side, the HTML arrives with no inline styles. The browser renders an unstyled page first, then re-renders after hydration — a visible Flash Of Unstyled Content (FOUC) on every first page load.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Guidelines for safe and maintainable CSS-in-JS usage:

list.item: Define styled.X at module scope. Never inside the component function — class explosion plus re-renders.
Use transient props ($variant, $size). Standard props leak to the DOM and trigger React warnings.
Co-locate tokens with TypeScript augmentation. A typed theme prevents 90 percent of "color undefined" bugs.
Memoize variant tables outside the component body so css blocks are stable references.
Extract critical CSS for SSR in client-only frameworks; without it, FOUC on first paint.
Prefer zero-runtime libs (vanilla-extract, linaria, panda-css) for SSR/RSC builds; runtime CSS-in-JS is a 2017 pattern aging out.
Cap dynamic interpolations. If a single component has more than 10 ${theme...} reads, refactor to CSS variables exposed by the theme provider.
Test theme switching via @testing-library/react with both providers; toggling missed theme keys often only show in dark mode.
Lint banned patterns (color: red, hardcoded hex codes, !important, :hover without :focus-visible) with stylelint inside template literals.
Track CSS-in-JS bundle weight. Add size-limit for both runtime and generated styles; alert on plus 5 KB gzip per release.
Server-safe pragma. For RSC partial support, mark interactive style branches with 'use client' files only and keep static markup in Server Components using Tailwind or Modules.

section.name: gotchas
section.p: Common pitfalls when using CSS-in-JS libraries:

list.item: Default LLM output picks styled-components. Models trained on 2019-2022 React tutorials emit it by default; in 2026 Next.js App Router that's broken. Always pin the prompt to the framework plus RSC awareness.
styled.div inside the component. Agents put styled definitions inside the function body, generating a new class on every render. Lint and reject.
Missing 'use client'. Agents drop styled-components into Server Components without the directive. Compile-time fail or hydration mismatch. Mandate the directive.
Theme typing not augmented. Agents generate theme.colors.primary but skip declare module styled-components. Require it in the bundle.
Mixing Emotion css prop with styled-components. Both libs export css; the agent imports the wrong one and templates run as JS. Pin imports explicitly.
No transient prop convention. Agents use variant not $variant; React warns about unknown DOM attribute. Force $ prefix.
Theme drift between agents. One agent uses theme.spacing.md, another uses theme.space.md; over a session the theme schema mutates. Lock the theme TS interface and use it as the contract.
Hardcoded hex codes in templates. Default LLM behavior for "primary blue" is #3b82f6. Bypasses the token system. Reject literal colors outside theme/tokens.ts.
Ignoring SSR wiring. Agents build a styled component, ship it, and skip ServerStyleSheet (styled-components) or extractCriticalToChunks (Emotion). FOUC in production.
Dark mode bait-and-switch. Agents add a darkTheme but no provider switch; no actual dark mode. Pair with a ThemeProvider and a hook (useColorScheme), never just declare both objects.

Atomic CSS confusion. Agents conflate runtime CSS-in-JS with Tailwind and Panda. div className={styles.btn} (Modules) vs Btn $variant="x" (styled) vs div className={btn({variant:'x'})} (Panda). Keep one model per project.
Type-safety regressions on theme update. Renaming a token requires updating the augmentation interface; agents update tokens but not the type, producing silent stale typings until next compile.
CSS reset duplication. Agents add createGlobalStyle resets while a Tailwind @base reset already runs, triggering double specificity wars.

section.name: tooling-and-services
section.p: Recommended tools and services for CSS-in-JS development and production:

list.item: Storybook — mandatory for component preview. Agents can scaffold stories from styled component types.
Chromatic — SaaS visual regression testing. Catches CSS-in-JS regressions human review misses (subpixel, theme variants).
Tokens Studio (Figma plugin) — pull design tokens via JSON. Agents transform with style-dictionary.
Style Dictionary — OSS CLI pipeline output: TS, CSS variables, SCSS, native StyleSheet.
Panda CSS Studio — OSS dev tool to visualize generated atomic classes during agent generation.
Vercel and Netlify — deploy preview catches RSC plus runtime CSS-in-JS incompatibilities at build.
Compiled by Atlassian — build-time CSS-in-JS with React typings; agent-friendly.
Linaria CLI — compiles tagged template literals to atomic CSS at build.

template.purpose: Detects RSC/RN/Vite context and recommends or rejects CSS-in-JS lib
